text:
Choose the INCORRECT statement among the following:

A. The reactivity of aromatic aldehydes and ketones is less than that of aliphatic carbonyl compounds towards nucleophilic addition reactions.
B. Benzaldehyde does not give Fehling's test.
C. The H-atoms in ethanal are acidic in nature.
D. p-Nitrobenzaldehyde is less reactive than benzaldehyde towards nucleophilic addition reactions.
explanation:
**Statement A — Correct:** Aromatic groups are bulkier, causing steric hindrance at the sp² carbonyl carbon → reduced nucleophilic addition reactivity.

**Statement B — Correct:** Fehling's solution is a mild oxidising agent that cannot oxidise benzaldehyde (aromatic aldehydes don't react).

**Statement C — Correct:** Ethanal undergoes keto-enol tautomerism. The enol form has an acidic O—H, making the α-H atoms acidic.

**Statement D — INCORRECT:** p-Nitrobenzaldehyde is actually **MORE reactive** than benzaldehyde. The —NO₂ group exerts a −M (−R) effect, withdrawing electrons from the ring and increasing the partial positive charge on the carbonyl carbon, making it more susceptible to nucleophilic attack.
name: Questions Item 1

text: The main constituent of vinegar is
explanation: **Vinegar** is a dilute aqueous solution of **acetic acid** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}\)). It typically contains 5–8% acetic acid by volume. Vinegar is widely used in culinary arts as a condiment and food preservative. It is produced by fermentation of ethanol by acetic acid bacteria.
name: Questions Item 2

text: The product formed when formaldehyde reacts with ammonia is
explanation:
Formaldehyde (HCHO) reacts with ammonia to form **urotropine** (hexamethylenetetramine):

\[6\text{HCHO} + 4\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow (\text{CH}_2)_6\text{N}_4 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}\]

Urotropine is a cage-shaped molecule and has important applications as a urinary antiseptic, in medicine, and in the synthesis of explosives (RDX). It is also known as **hexamethylenetetramine** or methenamine.
name: Questions Item 3

text: What sized ring is formed when two carboxylic acid molecules form a dimeric structure through intermolecular hydrogen bonding? (Consider each hydrogen bond as a bond)
explanation:
In the carboxylic acid dimer, two molecules form **two intermolecular O—H...O hydrogen bonds**:

\[\text{R—C(=O)—OH...O=C(—OH)—R}\]

Counting all bonds in the ring: C–O (1), O–H (2), H...O (3), C=O (4), C (5), C=O (6), O (7), H–O (8)

The ring contains: C, O, H (H-bond), O, C, O, H (H-bond), O — **8 atoms/bonds** forming an **8-membered ring**.
name: Questions Item 4

text: Compound 'A' undergoes formation of cyanohydrin, which on hydrolysis gives lactic acid (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH}\)). Therefore, compound 'A' is
explanation:
Lactic acid is \(\text{CH}_3\text{—CH(OH)—COOH}\). Working backwards from lactic acid:

**Step 2 — Hydrolysis:** cyanohydrin \(\text{CH}_3\text{—CH(OH)—CN} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{CH}_3\text{—CH(OH)—COOH}\) (lactic acid)

**Step 1 — Cyanohydrin formation:** \(\text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{HCN} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{—CH(OH)—CN}\)

So compound A must be **acetaldehyde** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CHO}\)), which has a CH₃ group and a CHO group.
name: Questions Item 5

text:
The correct order of decreasing acid strength of the following acids is:

(1) Trichloroacetic acid
(2) Trifluoroacetic acid
(3) Acetic acid
(4) Formic acid
explanation:
Acid strength of carboxylic acids depends on the −I effect of substituents (electron-withdrawing stabilises carboxylate anion → stronger acid):

- **F** has highest −I effect (highest electronegativity), so **trifluoroacetic acid** is the strongest acid
- **Cl** has strong −I effect, so **trichloroacetic acid** is next
- **Formic acid** (HCOOH): no alkyl +I group, H is electron-withdrawing slightly
- **Acetic acid** (CH₃COOH): CH₃ has +I effect, weakens acid strength

Decreasing order: **\(\text{CF}_3\text{COOH} > \text{CCl}_3\text{COOH} > \text{HCOOH} > \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}\)**

= **2 > 1 > 4 > 3**
name: Questions Item 6

text: Which of the following compounds will give butanone on oxidation with alkaline \(\text{KMnO}_4\) solution?
options: None of the options are correct
explanation:
Butanone (butan-2-one, \(\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_2\text{CH}_3\)) is a **secondary ketone**.

- **Butan-1-ol** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}\)): primary alcohol → oxidises to butanal (aldehyde), then butanoic acid — **NOT butanone** ✗
- **Butan-2-ol** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH(OH)CH}_2\text{CH}_3\)): secondary alcohol → oxidises to **butanone (butan-2-one)** ✓

Alkaline \(\text{KMnO}_4\) oxidises secondary alcohols to ketones.
name: Questions Item 7

text:
The increasing order of acidic strength among the following compounds is:

I. Benzoic acid  II. 4-nitrobenzoic acid  III. 3,4-dinitrobenzoic acid  IV. 4-methoxybenzoic acid
explanation:
Acid strength of benzoic acid derivatives depends on substituent effects:

- **—OCH₃** (methoxy): +M effect (electron-donating) → destabilises carboxylate → **weakest acid** (IV)
- **Benzoic acid**: reference (I)
- **4-nitrobenzoic acid** (one —NO₂): −M effect → stabilises carboxylate → stronger acid (II)
- **3,4-dinitrobenzoic acid** (two —NO₂ groups): more −M effect → **strongest acid** (III)

Increasing order: **IV < I < II < III**
name: Questions Item 9

text:
The increasing order of boiling points of the following compounds is:

(i) \(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}\)  (ii) \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CHO}\)  (iii) \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}\)  (iv) \(\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_3\)
explanation:
Boiling points are determined by intermolecular forces, especially hydrogen bonding:

- **(ii) Propanal** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CHO}\)): no H-bonding, lowest BP
- **(iv) Acetone** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_3\)): no H-bonding but dipole-dipole interactions — slightly higher than propanal
- **(iii) 1-Propanol** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}\)): O—H hydrogen bonding → higher BP
- **(i) Acetic acid** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}\)): forms dimers through two H-bonds → highest BP

Increasing order: **(ii) < (iv) < (iii) < (i)**
name: Questions Item 10

text:
The products Q and R in the following reactions are:

\(\text{HC≡CCH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{Lindlar's catalyst, H}_2} P \xrightarrow{\text{(i) O}_3, \text{(ii) Zn/H}_2\text{O}} Q + R\)
explanation:
**Step 1 — Lindlar's catalyst reduction:**
\[\text{CH}_3\text{—C≡CH} + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Lindlar}} \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{—CH=CH}_2}_{P\text{ (propene, cis)}}\]

Lindlar's catalyst selectively reduces alkynes to cis-alkenes.

**Step 2 — Reductive ozonolysis of propene:**
\[\text{CH}_3\text{—CH=CH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{(i) O}_3, \text{(ii) Zn/H}_2\text{O}} \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{CHO}}_{Q\text{ (ethanal)}} + \underbrace{\text{HCHO}}_{R\text{ (methanal)}}\]

Q = **Ethanal** (acetaldehyde), R = **Methanal** (formaldehyde)
name: Questions Item 11

text: Formic acid is heated with conc. \(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\) at 100°C to form A and B. When \(\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3\) is heated strongly with B, the products formed are C and D. C can also be obtained by reacting \(\text{CaCO}_3\) with dil. HCl. What is D?
explanation:
**Step 1:** Formic acid dehydration with conc. \(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\):
\[\text{HCOOH} \xrightarrow{\text{Conc. H}_2\text{SO}_4, 100°C} \underbrace{\text{CO}}_{B} + \underbrace{\text{H}_2\text{O}}_{A}\]

**Step 2:** CO reduces iron oxide:
\[3\text{CO}(B) + \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow \underbrace{2\text{Fe}}_{D} + \underbrace{3\text{CO}_2}_{C}\]

**Verification:** C (\(\text{CO}_2\)) is also obtained by: \(\text{CaCO}_3 + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{CaCl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2\) ✓

D = **Fe (iron)**
name: Questions Item 12

text:
The product of the following reaction is:

\(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CHO} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CHO} \xrightarrow{\text{Dil. NaOH}, \Delta}\)
explanation:
This is a **cross aldol condensation** between benzaldehyde (no α-H, acts as electrophile) and propionaldehyde (has α-H, acts as nucleophile).

The α-carbon (C2) of propionaldehyde attacks the carbonyl carbon of benzaldehyde:

\[\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CHO} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CHO} \xrightarrow{\text{OH}^-} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH(OH)CH(CH}_3)\text{CHO}\]

On heating (\(\Delta\)), the β-hydroxy aldehyde **dehydrates** to give the α,β-unsaturated aldehyde:

\[\rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH=C(CH}_3)\text{CHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}\]

Product = **\(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH=C(CH}_3)\text{CHO}\)** (α-methylcinnamaldehyde)
name: Questions Item 13

text: What are the products formed when an aldehyde (RCHO) is reacted with Tollen's reagent?
explanation:
Tollen's reagent is \([\text{Ag(NH}_3)_2]^+\text{OH}^-\). The aldehyde is **oxidised** to carboxylate while silver ion is **reduced** to metallic silver (silver mirror):

\[\text{RCHO} + 2[\text{Ag(NH}_3)_2]^+\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{RCOO}^- + 2\text{Ag} + 4\text{NH}_3 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{H}^+\]

Products: **Ag** (silver mirror), **\(\text{H}_2\text{O}\)**, **\(\text{RCOO}^-\)** (carboxylate), **\(\text{NH}_3\)**
name: Questions Item 14

text:
Which of the following sets is in the correct order regarding the property mentioned?

I. \(\text{NCCH}_2\text{COOH} > \text{FCH}_2\text{COOH} > \text{H}_3\text{CCH}_2\text{COOH}\) (Acidity)

II. \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CHO} > \text{PhCOCH}_3 > \text{PhCHO}\) (Reactivity)

III. \(\text{H}_3\text{COCH}_2\text{CH}_3 < \text{H}_3\text{CCH}_2\text{CHO} < \text{H}_3\text{CCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}\) (Boiling points)
explanation:
**Set I — Correct:** Acidity ∝ −I effect. NC— has strong −I, F— has strong −I (CN > F in −I effect), while CH₃CH₂— has +I effect. Order: NCCH₂COOH > FCH₂COOH > CH₃CH₂COOH ✓

**Set II — Incorrect:** The correct reactivity order for nucleophilic addition is:
- Aldehydes > ketones
- Less steric hindrance → more reactive
- Resonance → less reactive
PhCHO (aromatic aldehyde, resonance stabilised) < CH₃CH₂CHO (aliphatic aldehyde) < PhCOCH₃ is wrong — PhCOCH₃ is a ketone, which is always less reactive than aldehydes. Correct order: CH₃CH₂CHO > PhCHO > PhCOCH₃ ✗

**Set III — Correct:** Boiling point order: ether < aldehyde < alcohol (due to increasing H-bonding ability). Methyl ethyl ether < propanal < 1-propanol ✓

Correct sets: **I and III**.
name: Questions Item 15

text:
Identify Z in the following sequence of reactions:

\(\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{Br}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{(i) Alc. KOH, (ii) NaNH}_2} X \xrightarrow{\text{Fe tube, 873 K}} Y \xrightarrow{\text{(CH}_3\text{CO)}_2\text{O/Anhy. AlCl}_3, \Delta} Z\)
explanation:
**Step 1:** \(\text{CH}_2\text{BrCH}_2\text{Br} \xrightarrow{\text{Alc. KOH}} \text{CH}_2\text{=CHBr} \xrightarrow{\text{NaNH}_2} \text{HC≡CH}\) (acetylene, X)

**Step 2:** \(3\text{HC≡CH} \xrightarrow{\text{Fe tube, 873 K}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_6\) (benzene, Y)

**Step 3:** Benzene + acetic anhydride with anhydrous AlCl₃ (Friedel-Crafts acylation):
\[\text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + (\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{AlCl}_3} \underbrace{\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COCH}_3}_{Z} + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}\]

Z = **Acetophenone**
name: Questions Item 16

text: What is the catalyst used in the reaction \(\text{H}_2\text{C=CH}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CHO}\) (aqueous medium)?
explanation:
The conversion of ethylene to acetaldehyde using oxygen in aqueous medium is known as the **Wacker process**:

\[\text{CH}_2\text{=CH}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{PdCl}_2, \text{CuCl}_2, \text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{CH}_3\text{CHO}\]

The catalyst is **PdCl₂ (Pd(II))** in combination with CuCl₂ as a co-catalyst. CuCl₂ re-oxidises Pd⁰ back to Pd²⁺, completing the catalytic cycle.
name: Questions Item 17

text:
\(\text{CH}_2\text{=CH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{(i) O}_3, \text{(ii) Zn/H}_2\text{O}} A \xrightarrow{\text{Conc. NaOH}} B + C\)

The reaction of A to give B and C is an example of
explanation:
**Step 1:** Ozonolysis of ethylene gives formaldehyde:
\[\text{CH}_2\text{=CH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{O}_3, \text{Zn/H}_2\text{O}} 2\text{HCHO}\text{ (A)}\]

**Step 2:** Formaldehyde has **no α-hydrogen**. In the presence of concentrated NaOH, it undergoes **self-oxidation-reduction (disproportionation)** — the Cannizzaro reaction:

\[2\text{HCHO} \xrightarrow{\text{Conc. NaOH}} \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}}_{B\text{ (methanol)}} + \underbrace{\text{HCOONa}}_{C\text{ (sodium formate)}}\]

This is the **Cannizzaro reaction** — characteristic of aldehydes lacking α-hydrogen.
name: Questions Item 18

text: Wurtz reaction of bromoethane gives n-butane. Sodium salt of X on heating with sodalime also gives n-butane. Compound X is
explanation:
n-Butane has the formula \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3\) (4 carbons).

Duma's reaction (sodium salt + sodalime = NaOH + CaO) decarboxylates the acid:

\[\text{R—COONa} + \text{NaOH} \xrightarrow{\text{CaO}, \Delta} \text{R—H} + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3\]

For product = n-butane (\(\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}\)), R must be \(\text{C}_4\text{H}_9\) (n-butyl):
\[\text{R—H} = \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3\]

So X must be **pentanoic acid** = \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}\) = **\(\text{CH}_3(\text{CH}_2)_3\text{COOH}\)**
name: Questions Item 19

text: Which one of the following is used for purification of aldehydes?
explanation:
**Sodium bisulphite (\(\text{NaHSO}_3\))** is used for purification of aldehydes:

\[\text{R—CHO} + \text{NaHSO}_3 \rightarrow \text{R—CH(OH)—SO}_3\text{Na} \downarrow\text{ (solid adduct)}\]

The aldehyde forms an insoluble solid bisulphite adduct, which is separated by filtration. Impurities that do not react remain in solution. The pure aldehyde is recovered by hydrolysis:

\[\text{Adduct} + \text{Dil. acid/Dil. NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Pure R—CHO} + \text{NaHSO}_3\]

Note: NaHSO₃ also works with methyl ketones and cyclic ketones.
name: Questions Item 21

text:
Identify the functional groups present in the resulting compound Y in the following sequence:

\(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{N}_2\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O, 283 K}} X \xrightarrow{\text{(i) CHCl}_3/\text{NaOH(aq), (ii) H}_3\text{O}^+} Y\)
explanation:
**Step 1:** Diazonium salt hydrolysis:
\[\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{N}_2\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O, 283 K}} \underbrace{\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH}}_{X\text{ (phenol)}}\]

**Step 2:** Reimer-Tiemann reaction (CHCl₃ + aq. NaOH):
\[\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{CHCl}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{(OH)(CHO)}\]

The Reimer-Tiemann reaction converts phenol to a **hydroxy benzaldehyde** (salicylaldehyde), introducing a formyl (—CHO) group ortho to the OH.

Y contains functional groups: **—OH** and **—CHO**
name: Questions Item 22

text: The reduction products of an aldehyde, ketone and carboxylic acid in the presence of lithium aluminium hydride are respectively X, Y and Z. What are X, Y and Z?
explanation:
LiAlH₄ (lithium aluminium hydride) is a powerful reducing agent that reduces all carbonyl-containing compounds:

- **Aldehyde** (\(\text{R—CHO}\)) + LiAlH₄ → \(\text{R—CH}_2\text{OH}\) **(primary alcohol, X)** ✓
- **Ketone** (\(\text{R—CO—R'}\)) + LiAlH₄ → \(\text{R—CH(OH)—R'}\) **(secondary alcohol, Y)** ✓
- **Carboxylic acid** (\(\text{R—COOH}\)) + LiAlH₄ → \(\text{R—CH}_2\text{OH}\) **(primary alcohol, Z)** ✓

X = R—CH₂OH, Y = R—CH(OH)—R', Z = R—CH₂OH
name: Questions Item 23

text: The correct set of reagents (X, Y, Z) required to convert benzene to m-nitrobenzoic acid are
options: X = CO/HCl/anhy. AlCl₃; Y = KMnO₄; Z = LiAlH₄
X = HNO₃+H₂SO₄; Y = Br₂/AlCl₃; Z = KCN/H₃O⁺
X = Br₂/AlCl₃; Y = HNO₃+H₂SO₄; Z = KCN/H₃O⁺
X = CO/HCl/anhy. AlCl₃; Y = KMnO₄; Z = Conc. HNO₃ + Conc. H₂SO₄
explanation:
To make **m-nitrobenzoic acid**, the nitro group must be in the meta position. The —COOH group is a meta-director:

**Strategy:** First introduce —CHO (meta-director), then nitrate (→ meta product), then oxidise —CHO to —COOH.

**Step 1 (X):** Gattermann-Koch formylation: Benzene + CO + HCl + anhyd. AlCl₃ → **Benzaldehyde**

**Step 2 (Y):** Oxidation: Benzaldehyde + KMnO₄ → **Benzoic acid** (—CHO becomes —COOH, a meta-director)

**Step 3 (Z):** Nitration: Benzoic acid + Conc. HNO₃ + Conc. H₂SO₄ → **m-Nitrobenzoic acid**

X = CO/HCl/AlCl₃, Y = KMnO₄, Z = Conc. HNO₃ + Conc. H₂SO₄
name: Questions Item 24

text: Ozonolysis of hex-2-ene (\(\text{Hex-2-ene} \xrightarrow{\text{O}_3} \text{Ozonide} \xrightarrow{\text{Zn+H}_2\text{O}} X + Y\)) gives X and Y as
explanation:
Hex-2-ene: \(\text{CH}_3\text{—CH=CH—CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3\)

Ozonolysis cleaves at the C2=C3 double bond:

\[\underbrace{\text{CH}_3}_{\text{left}} \underbrace{\text{—CH=CH—CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3}_{\text{right}}\]

- Left fragment → \(\underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{CHO}}_{Y\text{ (ethanal)}}\)
- Right fragment → \(\underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CHO}}_{X\text{ (butanal)}}\)

X = **butanal (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CHO}\))**, Y = **ethanal (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CHO}\))**
name: Questions Item 25

text:
A, B and C in the following reaction:

Isopropyl chloride \(\xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} A \xrightarrow{\text{Cu/573 K}} B\); \(B \xrightarrow{\text{NaOI}} \text{Iodoform} + C\)
explanation:
**Step 1:** Isopropyl chloride + NaOH → **A** = propan-2-ol (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH(OH)CH}_3\)) (nucleophilic substitution)

**Step 2:** Propan-2-ol + Cu at 573 K (dehydrogenation) → **B** = acetone (\(\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_3\))

**Step 3:** Acetone + NaOI (sodium hypoiodite — iodoform reaction):
\[\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_3 + 3\text{NaOI} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{CHI}_3}_{\text{iodoform}} + \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}}_{C}\]

A = propan-2-ol, B = acetone, C = **\(\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}\)** (sodium acetate)
name: Questions Item 26

text:
What are X and Y from ozonolysis of pent-2-ene?

\(\text{Pent-2-ene} \xrightarrow{\text{(i) O}_3, \text{(ii) Zn/H}_2\text{O}} X + Y\)
explanation:
Pent-2-ene: \(\text{CH}_3\text{—CH=CH—CH}_2\text{CH}_3\)

Ozonolysis cleaves the C2=C3 double bond:
- Left fragment: \(\text{CH}_3\text{—CHO}\) = **ethanal (acetaldehyde, X)**
- Right fragment: \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{—CHO}\) = **propanal (Y)**

X = **\(\text{CH}_3\text{CHO}\)** (ethanal), Y = **\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CHO}\)** (propanal)
name: Questions Item 27

text: The hydrogen atom bonded to the carbon designated by which number in \(\overset{4}{\text{CH}}_3\overset{3}{\text{CH}}_2\overset{2}{\text{CH}}_2\overset{1}{\text{CHO}}\) is most acidic?
explanation:
In an aldehyde, the α-carbon (the carbon directly adjacent to the carbonyl group C=O) carries the most acidic hydrogen atoms. This is because:

1. The carbonyl C=O is strongly electron-withdrawing (−I and −M effects)
2. The α-H is stabilised upon removal as the **enolate ion** is resonance-stabilised

In \(\text{CH}_3\text{—CH}_2\text{—CH}_2\text{—CHO}\):
- C-1 = carbonyl carbon (the CHO carbon itself — no H on carbonyl)
- **C-2** = α-carbon directly next to CHO → **most acidic H** ✓
- C-3 = β-carbon → less acidic
- C-4 = least acidic
name: Questions Item 28

text:
The order of strengths of the following carboxylic acids is:

(i) \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}\)  (ii) \(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}\)  (iii) \(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOH}\)  (iv) \(\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}\) (phenylacetic acid)
explanation:
Acid strength is determined by electron-withdrawing (−I) and electron-donating (+I) effects:

- **(iii) Benzoic acid** (\(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOH}\)): phenyl is electron-withdrawing via −I and −M effects → **strongest** in this group
- **(iv) Phenylacetic acid** (\(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}\)): phenyl is one carbon away → weaker −I effect than direct phenyl attachment
- **(ii) Acetic acid** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}\)): CH₃ has mild +I effect
- **(i) Propionic acid** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}\)): longer alkyl chain, stronger +I effect → **weakest acid**

Order: **(iii) > (iv) > (ii) > (i)**
name: Questions Item 29

text: Methane of the polluted air reacts with ozone and forms
explanation:
The question refers to methane reacting with **oxygen in polluted air** (combustion), not specifically with ozone alone:

\[\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}\]

This is the complete **combustion** of methane. The products are **\(\text{CO}_2\)** (carbon dioxide) and **\(\text{H}_2\text{O}\)** (water).
name: Questions Item 30

text:
In the reaction sequence: \(\text{Hexane} \xrightarrow{\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3, 773\text{K}} \text{Benzene} \xrightarrow{\text{Anhy. AlCl}_3} \text{Friedel-Crafts acylation with CH}_3\text{COCl} \rightarrow Y\)

Y is
explanation:
The reaction sequence:

**Step 1:** Hexane → Benzene (aromatisation/dehydrocyclisation with Cr₂O₃ at 773 K)

**Step 2:** Friedel-Crafts acylation of benzene with acetyl chloride (CH₃COCl) and anhydrous AlCl₃:

\[\text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + \text{CH}_3\text{COCl} \xrightarrow{\text{Anhy. AlCl}_3} \underbrace{\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COCH}_3}_{Y} + \text{HCl}\]

Y = **Acetophenone** (\(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COCH}_3\))
name: Questions Item 31

text:
The correct statements about aspirin (Z in: phenol → salicylic acid via Kolbe's reaction → Z via acetylation) from the following are:

I. It is o-hydroxybenzoic acid.
II. It is a non-narcotic analgesic.
III. It acts as antipyretic.
IV. It acts as antihistamine.
explanation:
Z is **aspirin** (acetylsalicylic acid), formed by acetylation of salicylic acid.

Checking statements:
- **Statement I — Incorrect:** Aspirin is NOT o-hydroxybenzoic acid. That's salicylic acid. Aspirin is **o-acetoxybenzoic acid** (the OH is acetylated to OC(=O)CH₃).
- **Statement II — Correct:** Aspirin is a **non-narcotic analgesic** (relieves pain without causing addiction/narcosis).
- **Statement III — Correct:** Aspirin acts as an **antipyretic** (fever reducer).
- **Statement IV — Incorrect:** Aspirin is NOT an antihistamine. Antihistamines block histamine receptors.

Correct statements: **II and III**
name: Questions Item 32

text: The compound which does NOT respond to the iodoform test is
explanation:
**Iodoform test** is positive for:
- Compounds with a **CH₃CO—** group (methyl ketones)
- **Acetaldehyde** (CH₃CHO)
- **Secondary alcohols** that can be oxidised to methyl ketones (e.g., \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH(OH)CH}_3\) → acetone)

Checking options:
- **(a) CH₃CHO**: has CH₃—C(=O)— → **positive** ✓
- **(b) CH₃CH(OH)CH₃**: oxidises to acetone (CH₃COCH₃) → **positive** ✓
- **(c) C₂H₅COC₂H₅** (diethyl ketone): has **no methyl group** adjacent to C=O → **NO iodoform test** ✓
- **(d) C₆H₅COCH₃** (acetophenone): has CH₃CO— → **positive** ✓
name: Questions Item 33

text:
What are X and Y in the following reactions?

Reaction 1: \(\text{R—CH}_2\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Cu/573 K}} X\)

Reaction 2: \(\text{CH}_3\text{C(CH}_3)_2\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Cu/573 K}} Y\)
explanation:
Passing vapours of alcohols over heated copper at 573 K:

**Primary alcohols → aldehydes (dehydrogenation):**
\[\text{R—CH}_2\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Cu/573 K}} \underbrace{\text{R—CHO}}_{X} + \text{H}_2\]

**Tertiary alcohols → alkenes (dehydration):**
Tertiary alcohols cannot be dehydrogenated (no α-H on the same carbon as OH). Instead they **dehydrate** to alkenes:
\[\text{CH}_3\text{C(CH}_3)_2\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Cu/573 K}} \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{C(=CH}_2)\text{CH}_3}_{Y} + \text{H}_2\text{O}\]

X = **R—CHO** (aldehyde), Y = **\(\text{CH}_3\text{C(=CH}_2)\text{CH}_3\)** (2-methylpropene)
name: Questions Item 34

text:
X and Y in the following reaction sequence are:

\(\text{RMgX} \xrightarrow{\text{CdCl}_2} X \xrightarrow{\text{R'COCl}} Y\)
explanation:
Grignard reagents react with cadmium chloride to form **dialkylcadmium compounds**, which then react with acid chlorides to give **ketones** (avoiding overreaction that would occur with Grignard reagents):

**Step 1:**
\[2\text{RMgX} + \text{CdCl}_2 \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{R}_2\text{Cd}}_{X} + 2\text{MgXCl}\]

**Step 2:**
\[\text{R}_2\text{Cd} + 2\text{R'COCl} \rightarrow 2\underbrace{\text{R'C(=O)R}}_{Y\text{ (ketone)}} + \text{CdCl}_2\]

X = **R₂Cd** (dialkylcadmium), Y = **R'COR** (ketone)
name: Questions Item 35

text: Order of acidity of benzoic acid (I), 4-methoxybenzoic acid (II), acetic acid (III) and 4-nitrobenzoic acid (IV) is
explanation:
Acid strength depends on electron effects on the carboxylate anion stabilisation:

- **IV (4-nitrobenzoic acid)**: —NO₂ is a powerful EWG (−M and −I) → stabilises carboxylate → **strongest acid**
- **I (Benzoic acid)**: phenyl is electron-withdrawing via −I effect → stronger than acetic acid
- **II (4-methoxybenzoic acid)**: —OCH₃ is an EDG (+M) → destabilises carboxylate → weaker than benzoic acid
- **III (Acetic acid)**: CH₃ has +I effect → **weakest acid** in this group

Order: **IV > I > II > III**
name: Questions Item 36

text: Butanone reacts with methyl magnesium bromide to form an addition product (Z). On hydrolysis, Z gives
explanation:
Butanone (\(\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_2\text{CH}_3\)) reacts with \(\text{CH}_3\text{MgBr}\) (Grignard reagent) via nucleophilic addition:

\[\text{CH}_3\text{—CO—CH}_2\text{CH}_3 + \text{CH}_3\text{MgBr} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{—C(OMgBr)(CH}_3)\text{—CH}_2\text{CH}_3\]

Hydrolysis with dilute HCl:

\[\rightarrow \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{C(OH)(CH}_3)\text{CH}_3}_{\text{2-methylbutan-2-ol}}\]

Product = **2-methylbutan-2-ol** = \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{C(OH)(CH}_3)\text{CH}_3\)
name: Questions Item 37

text: Reagents used in Etard reaction (I) and Stephen reaction (II) are:
explanation:
**Etard reaction (I):** Oxidation of a methyl group on an arene (toluene) to an aldehyde using **chromyl chloride (CrO₂Cl₂)**:
\[\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_3 + 2\text{CrO}_2\text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow [\text{brown complex}] \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CHO}\]

**Stephen reaction (II):** Reduction of a nitrile to an aldehyde using **SnCl₂/HCl** in ether:
\[\text{R—CN} \xrightarrow{\text{SnCl}_2/\text{HCl}} \text{R—CH=NH·HCl} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{R—CHO} + \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}\]

Etard = **CrO₂Cl₂**, Stephen = **SnCl₂/HCl**
name: Questions Item 39
